name: Challis
short_description: BLM public land near Challis offering easy agate and chalcedony collecting on moderate slopes at 5,895 ft elevation. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit.
description:
The Challis area in Custer County sits within Idaho's rich mineralogical region where Miocene-era volcanic and hydrothermal activity created abundant silica deposits. This BLM public land offers rockhounders accessible terrain with moderate slopes ideal for surface collecting. The volcanic geology here has concentrated agate, chalcedony, and quartz specimens in weathered outcrops and wash gravels, making it productive for both beginners and experienced collectors.

The elevation of nearly 6,000 feet means the area experiences distinct seasonal changes, with spring snowmelt and summer rains exposing fresh material in drainages and eroded hillsides. Walking the washes and scanning slope surfaces often yields colorful agate fragments and chalcedony nodules. The terrain is rocky but not treacherous, with good footing for careful rockhounding in most conditions.

As BLM land, this location requires no permit and welcomes personal use collectors. The 25-pound daily limit per person encourages sustainable collecting while allowing you to take home quality specimens. The moderate slope makes this site suitable for families and rockhounds seeking casual collecting without extreme hiking demands. Bring plenty of water as the high elevation and open terrain offer limited shade.
history: The Challis area sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene-age volcanic ash flows and silica-rich hydrothermal fluids deposited extensive chalcedony and agate beds. This geology mirrors mining activity throughout central Idaho, where prospectors historically focused on precious metals but encountered abundant silica-based gemstone material in the process.
tips: Walk the bottoms and sides of dry washes where water erosion naturally concentrates lighter agate fragments and polished chalcedony pieces. Use a hand lens to spot translucent chalcedony nodules and banded agate on hillside surfaces; the morning light makes color contrasts more visible. Bring a small rock hammer or chisel to test specimens in place before collecting, as weathered exteriors often hide better color and patterns inside. Focus on the crumbly volcanic-derived soils where pieces weather out naturally rather than attempting to extract material from intact outcrops.
terrain_notes: Moderate slope terrain at 5,895 ft elevation with scattered sagebrush and small conifers. Footing is rocky with loose talus and gravel common. Walking distance from parking varies but most productive areas are within 0.5 to 1.5 miles of road access. Terrain is manageable for steady walkers but requires sturdy boots for ankle support on uneven ground.
best_season: June - September for optimal collecting. Winter snow at this elevation can block road access and make hiking difficult December through March. Spring (May) and early fall (October) are possible but weather unpredictable.
written_directions: From Custer, Idaho, head north on ID-75 toward Challis for approximately 30 miles. Watch for BLM access points and informal pullouts on the east and west sides of the highway where the terrain transitions to open sagebrush slopes. GPS coordinates 44.494013, -114.299897 mark a typical productive area accessible via short hikes from roadside parking. Park safely off the pavement and scan the immediate slopes and wash drainages nearby.
road_conditions: ID-75 is paved and well-maintained. Most productive collecting areas require short walks from pullouts; no high-clearance vehicle needed for highway access.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and wide shoulder areas along ID-75. No developed parking lot; expect 1-3 vehicle capacity at most pullouts. Park completely off pavement.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No site disturbance beyond surface collecting. Pack out all trash.
rules:

Pack out all trash
Respect all posted closures and seasonal restrictions
nearest_services: Custer, Idaho, approximately 30 miles south. Limited supplies; Challis, Idaho (approximately 40 miles north) offers more services including gas, food, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain with moderate slope. Short walks from parking (0.5-1.5 miles) over uneven rocky ground. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots recommended for ankle support. Suitable for most adults and older children with reasonable fitness.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Challis, Idaho?
faq.answer: The Challis BLM area is known for agate, chalcedony, quartz, and jasper. These silica-based gemstones occur as weathered nodules and fragments in volcanic soils and wash gravels. Agate specimens often display banding and color variation from the area's Miocene hydrothermal deposits.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Challis on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use collecting on BLM public land near Challis. You must follow the 25-pound daily limit per person and collect only for personal use, not commercial sale. Commercial operations require special use authorization from the BLM.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect agate and chalcedony near Challis?
faq.answer: June through September offers the best collecting conditions at this 5,895-foot elevation. Winter snow typically blocks access December through March. Spring (May) and early fall (October) are possible but weather can be unpredictable. Summer provides reliable access and better visibility for spotting specimens.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Challis suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Challis is beginner and family-friendly. The terrain is mostly moderate slope with short walks from roadside access, suitable for children ages 6 and older. Surface collecting requires no special skills, and specimens are visible on hillsides and in washes, making it ideal for learning rockhounding fundamentals.

faq.question: What tools and gear should I bring for rockhounding in the Challis area?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer or chisel, hand lens for identifying specimens, sturdy hiking boots for rocky footing, and plenty of water. Sunscreen and a hat are essential at the high elevation. A backpack for carrying specimens, collection bags, and a GPS device are helpful. Early morning visits provide better lighting for spotting chalcedony's translucence.

faq.question: How far is it from town services to the Challis rockhounding area?
faq.answer: Custer is approximately 30 miles south and has limited supplies. Challis is about 40 miles north and offers better access to gas, food, and lodging. The rockhounding areas are accessed directly from ID-75, so plan for a full day trip and bring all water and snacks you'll need, as services are sparse near the collecting sites.
